title: Awakening
narrativeIntro:
You open your eyes to soft light. It's not sunlight -- it's something cooler, more diffused, like dawn filtered through deep water. You're floating, or close to it. The space around you is vast and formless, a watercolor indigo that stretches in every direction.

There are structures here. You can feel them more than see them -- shapes at the edge of perception, dormant, waiting. They're built in a language you don't understand. Not yet.

The first step is always the same: name what you see.
narrativeOutro:
Five small lights where there were none. It doesn't seem like much, but look at the map -- where everything was dark, something now responds to you. The Drift noticed. You named things, and naming things is the first act of understanding.

Rest. Tomorrow, the patterns get more complex.
phmConnectionNote: Like Ryland, you wake up with no context. Everything is unfamiliar. You start by naming what you see.

challenges.title: The First Signal
challenges.narrativeHook: A symbol pulses faintly on the nearest surface. What does it represent?
challenges.instructions:
Read the code below carefully. Without running it, predict what `console.log(result)` will output.

The variable `signal` holds a value. The `typeof` operator is applied to it. What does the console respond?
challenges.solution:
// No code changes — this is a prediction challenge.
// The answer is: "number"
challenges.predictPrompt: What will be logged to the console?

explainGate.prompt: What does the typeof operator return, and why is it a string?

hints: typeof returns a string describing the type of a value — not the value itself.
typeof 42 doesn't return the number. It returns a word.
Numbers are described by the word "number". That's what typeof returns.

challenges.title: Naming Things
challenges.narrativeHook: The console responds to names. Give this value an identity.
challenges.instructions:
The system has detected three signals but they haven't been named. Declare the variables as instructed in the comments.

- `station` should hold `"Drift-7"` — this value might change later.
- `crew` should hold `1` — this will never change.
- `status` should hold `undefined` — the value isn't known yet, but it will be once the system wakes up.

tests.description: station should be declared with let (reassignable)
tests.testCode: try { station = 'test'; station = 'Drift-7'; } catch(e) { throw new Error('station should be reassignable — use let, not const'); }

tests.description: crew should be a constant (not reassignable)
tests.testCode: try { crew = 2; throw new Error('crew should not be reassignable — use const, not let'); } catch(e) { if (e.message.includes('should not')) throw e; }
if (status !== undefined) throw new Error(`Expected undefined, got ${status}`);

explainGate.prompt: When would you use let vs const?

hints: Use 'let' for values that might change, 'const' for values that won't.
You can explicitly assign undefined to a variable: let x = undefined;
The pattern: const crew = 1; for fixed values. let station = "Drift-7"; for changeable ones.

challenges.title: The Corrupted Label
challenges.narrativeHook: Something was named wrong. The system rejects it. Find the error.
challenges.instructions:
This code tries to update the station's name, but it crashes. The system throws an error on the second line because a naming rule was broken.

Find and fix the single bug. The station name should successfully update to `"Drift-9"`.

tests.testCode: if (typeof stationName === 'undefined') throw new Error('stationName was never declared');

explainGate.prompt: Why did using const cause the error?

hints: Read the error message carefully — what does 'Assignment to constant variable' tell you?
const means the variable can never be reassigned after its initial value is set.
Change const to let on the first line. That's the only change needed.

challenges.title: Fixed and Fluid
challenges.narrativeHook: Two values. The system asks which one can change and which is fixed.
challenges.instructions:
Declare the following four variables with the correct keyword — `let` or `const` — based on whether the value should be changeable.

- `maxFuel` — holds `100`, never changes
- `currentFuel` — starts at `100`, can decrease over time
- `engineRunning` — starts as `false`, can be toggled
- `shipName` — holds `"Hail Mary"`, never changes

The system will test both the values and whether each variable is correctly fixed or fluid.

tests.testCode: if (maxFuel !== 100) throw new Error(`maxFuel should be 100, got ${maxFuel}`); try { maxFuel = 0; throw new Error('maxFuel should be constant — use const'); } catch(e) { if (e.message.includes('should be')) throw e; }

if (currentFuel !== 100) throw new Error(`currentFuel should start at 100, got ${currentFuel}`); try { currentFuel = 50; currentFuel = 100; } catch(e) { throw new Error('currentFuel should be reassignable — use let, not const'); }

tests.description: engineRunning should be false and reassignable
tests.testCode: if (engineRunning !== false) throw new Error(`engineRunning should start as false, got ${engineRunning}`); try { engineRunning = true; engineRunning = false; } catch(e) { throw new Error('engineRunning should be reassignable — use let, not const'); }

tests.description: shipName should be 'Hail Mary' and constant
tests.testCode: if (shipName !== 'Hail Mary') throw new Error(`shipName should be 'Hail Mary', got '${shipName}'`); try { shipName = 'x'; throw new Error('shipName should be constant — use const'); } catch(e) { if (e.message.includes('should be')) throw e; }

hints: If a value should never change after being set, use const. If it needs to be updated later, use let.
Two of these are fixed (const) and two are fluid (let). Which values does the description say can change?
const maxFuel = 100; and const shipName = "Hail Mary"; are fixed. let currentFuel = 100; and let engineRunning = false; are fluid.

challenges.title: Echo
challenges.narrativeHook: You type a name. The system echoes something back. What will it say?
challenges.instructions:
Read the code below. Two lines will be logged to the console. Predict both.

Pay close attention to what happens when a string meets a number through the `+` operator. The system doesn't always do what you expect.
challenges.predictPrompt: What two lines will the console respond with?
predictChoices: Error: cannot add string and number

explainGate.prompt: Why is the result a string even though count is a number?

hints: When you use + between a string and a number, JavaScript doesn't refuse. It converts one of them.
The number becomes part of the string. The result of string + anything is always a string.
So typeof message will be "string" — the number 5 was absorbed into the text.
